vulnerability.title: Disable Access to Network bpf() Syscall From Unprivileged Processes
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled=1 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled = 1
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done #

triage_decision.reason: The remediation involves setting a kernel sysctl parameter and ensuring persistence via /etc/sysctl.d, which is a standard, low-risk configuration change that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical services.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We must plan remediation. We'll modify a sysctl config file ensuring persistence. Find existing config files that may set kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled. We need to comment out any occurrences in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf, /run/sysctl.d/*.conf, /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf unless symlink to /etc/sysctl.conf. Then add line to file /etc/sysctl.d/99-unprivileged_bpf_disabled.conf perhaps. And apply runtime with sysctl -w kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled=1. Approach: 1) Read /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf recognized. Use sed to comment out any lines that set kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled. Actually easiest: create new file /etc/sysctl.d/99-unprivileged_bpf_disabled.conf with line kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled = 1. But if there were conflicting lines, we should comment them. So do for each file. We'll read each file path. For each, we can run command to comment out lines. But can't chain. We can just use sed -i to comment out lines containing 'kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled' not already commented. But we need to avoid symlinks. We'll check readlink -f before editing. But cut to instructions: we can modify config file via sed -i 's/^\s*kernel\.unprivileged_bpf_disabled\s*=\s*[^#]/#&/'? Actually we want to comment out lines not commented. Use sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*kernel\.unprivileged_bpf_disabled[[:space:]]*=.*$/# &/'.

But we should not duplicate lines. And we maintain minimal. It's acceptable.

Plan: 1) Create new file /etc/sysctl.d/99-unprivileged_bpf_disabled.conf with content kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled = 1. 2) For each file pattern in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf, /run/sysctl.d/*.conf, /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf, if not symlink to /etc/sysctl.conf, edit using sed to comment out any non-comment lines that set kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled. Use read_file to check, then write_file with modified content.

We also need to set runtime: run 'sysctl -w kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled=1'.

Plan steps definitions.

We need to call review_plan.

Let's craft plan_description.

review_feedback: The remediation loop will comment out the line in the newly created persistence file, rendering the setting ineffective after reboot. While the runtime change is applied safely and will not disrupt critical services such as sshd, the fix will not persist, potentially allowing the vulnerability to reappear after a restart. The script’s handling of file permissions with write_file and its generic sed pattern may also produce unintended changes to existing sysctl configuration files.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will successfully enforce the kernel parameter and make it persistent, but it contains a few implementation mistakes that could negate the fix or create secondary issues.
concerns: The cleanup loop iterates over all *.conf files, including the newly created 99-unprivileged_bpf_disabled.conf. As written it will comment out the line in this file, making the change ineffective.
Using both an in-place sed edit and then an overwrite with write_file is redundant and risks changing file ownership/permissions if write_file defaults to root:root.
The regular expression anchors to the start of the line, so any lines where the setting appears after comments or other leading text will not be commented out, potentially leaving overrides active.
The path matching for symlinks only checks against /etc/sysctl.conf; other systemd-sysctl symlinks might get processed unintentionally.
No check is performed to ensure sysctl –w actually succeeded before proceeding to persistence.
suggested_improvements: Exclude the file /etc/sysctl.d/99-unprivileged_bpf_disabled.conf from the cleaning loop to avoid commenting out the directive you just added.
Remove the extra write_file step after sed – the in-place edit is sufficient and preserves ownership/permissions.
Use a more comprehensive regex such as 's/^[[:space:]]*kernel\.unprivileged_bpf_disabled\s*=.*$/# &/' that also handles cases where the directive follows a comment character on the same line.
Add logic to skip all systemd-sysctl symlinks (e.g., those ending in 'sysctl.d/*.conf') or check file metadata before editing.
Verify that sysctl -w returns 0 before creating the persistence file; if it fails, log an appropriate warning or retry.
